By that point, the customer had already lost confidence in the stability of the solution and decided not to proceed.

Ultimately, the cancellation was driven more by a loss of trust during the setup process than by technical feasibility.

The customer decided to cancel, noting that Method is not designed as a sales-first CRM. They faced challenges with account assignment, multi-currency reporting, and limited sales data visualization. While Method can address some of these needs through customization, the customer preferred a system that offered stronger sales features out-of-the-box.

The customer evaluated Nutshell and found it provided a better immediate sales experience, particularly around pipelines, dashboards, and reporting visibility.

Their needs: 

Stage 1: Purchase Order: generally where orders come in usually here is where we have to check that their pricing from the pourchase order is correct with our current pricing. Our pricing generally changes once a year and is based on volume. If we could have some sort of pricing rules built in to reflect that, that would be great.
 
Stage 2: Invoice: Usually if there is a price change we then send a final invoice to the customer for approval.

Stage 3: Payment: some of our customers require payment up front, others we bill after the product has shipped.

Stage 4: Custom Printing: some of our products have the option for custom printing. Generally, the process of this goes:

                Customer sends us their logo

                Logo created into a proof

                Send to customer for approval

               Having steps for each of these and even a way to send the proof where they can just click accept or reject with comments, would be great.

Stage 5: Fulfillment: this is where orders are filled in our warehouse

Stage 6: Shipping:  once the order is fulfilled, we are notified it is ready to ship where we then arrange for a truck to come pick it up or if smaller use standard mail to ship it out

Stage 7: Send Bill: if the customer does not require prepayment, we would send the invoice once the product has been shipped.

I called the customer and discussed the catalog for the Portal of Customers and Sub-customers. I informed them that, out of the box, the catalog will overwrite the custom portal screens they previously paid for. If they want to retain their existing customizations while still adding the catalog functionality, this requires a PPU agreement so our team can make the necessary adjustments.

I also explained that the actual build time for the Portal of Customers and Sub-customers is 3–4 hours, whereas with the catalog, the customer would only be charged for 1 hour—a significant cost savings. Despite this, the customer declined, stating they are not willing to pay anything additional. They blamed us, arguing that since the functionality already exists, it should be provided at no cost.

I reiterated in detail that while the functionality exists, customization is required to preserve their unique screens, and that customization falls outside of the standard setup. However, it was clear the customer had already made up their mind not to continue with Method CRM and is now using the catalog as an excuse to push for free work.

We were assessing with a customer and they decided on a different path.

The primary reasons that I am going to stay with the starter version of Salesforce.com with a possible move to a bigger version if more of our company decides to use it are:

1. Method bases everything off the contact.  We need everything based off the company with the ability to add multiple contacts to that company and opportunities related to that company. 
- I added many fields to the company profile, but the only way to see them was by going to the edit function.  There is a way to add them to the contact field, but that was clunky to me. 
2. The reporting in SFDC is much easier to customize. 
3. SFDC is multi-lingual.
4. The company, contact and opportunity screens are much easier to customize and adding fields is easier in SFDC. 
- It was difficult to change the items in a picklist in Method. 
5. The starter version of SFDC is $25/month paid annually.  That isn’t a major factor, but the starter version works for us right now. 
6. I didn’t need a lot of custom support, but I had pretty much tapped out my knowledge of Method with the customizations I did.  Anything more would need to be paid services.  I am able to customize SFDC and create reports without any paid support. 

Honestly, I am probably biased from the start because I have been using SFDC since 2005 with 3 different companies. Prior to that I was the Sales Automation Manager at a large company where I led the implementation of a CRM solution to 300+ sales reps.  After that, I spent several years selling CRM solutions and consulting services.  So, I was able to be involved in the customization and user adoption of the SFDC applications at the companies I worked for that used it.  I am obviously more familiar with SFDC and I understand it very well.  But I believe I did give Method an honest review and trial.  The issues above where the primary reasons I don’t believe it would work for us.

Hello-I ordered about $1k of customization, primarily to mimic some stuff we could do in QB Desktop that we couldn't do in QB Online (Client migrated to QBO last year); the team was responsive and did what we asked, but once we were using Method in practice, it was too buggy / cumbersome and kept requiring more and more customization.

If we were willing to spend more money it may have gotten to a usable state, but we were limited in what we were going to pay (see below) and felt like we were trying to force it.  We ended up switching to a purpose-built contractor solution instead.

Biggest dissatisfier was the fact that estimate line items were stuck in place once created; we routinely get asked to add items to estimates, that would include inserting rows between other rows/rearranging line items; it happened frequently enough that it couldn't be ignored.  This is feature is available in QBO, but not Method.  When i asked, it was offered as another customization.

I was paying the customization myself on behalf of client thinking this would save him money in long run, but it was so frustrating for him i ended up eating cost personally-not something I can absorb too frequently.

Team was friendly and tried to be helpful; i appreciate them ....but I should have done more due diligence up front on the tool than hoping that a limited amount of customizations would get us where we needed to be.

The setup process, which was promised to be seamless, turned out to be anything but. Despite assurances from Harsh that the CRM could cater to all our needs, the reality was far from it. Our initial complementary call with the designated setup assistant proved to be a significant letdown, as it became evident that he did not comprehend our requirements. Consequently, the work completed was not aligned with our explicit instructions. On top of that further assistance would be billable at an hourly rate, an arrangement that felt far from ideal given the poor handling of the complimentary setup.

We encountered additional challenges with several leads struggling to accept proposals sent through the Method platform and make payments for services. Attempting to address these issues with the support team or Harsh proved to be equally frustrating. Our attempts to seek resolution were met with either outright neglect or unhelpful responses, leaving us feeling unsupported and exacerbating the negative impact on our company's image.

Given the substantial issues we faced, coupled with the lack of responsive support, we no longer had confidence in using Method to manage our leads and clients. The only decision we felt comfortable making was to discontinue our reliance on Method CRM.

In summary, our experience has been marked by unmet promises, inadequate support, and a significant negative impact on our business operations. We hope this feedback prompts a reconsideration of your service delivery and support processes.

2.	Similarly, when a client receives an email confirmation after a payment is received in Method and they click on “View Payment” in their email, it still says a balance is owed. It takes 15+ minutes to reflect a $0.00 balance owed in the payment receipt (not Quickbooks, but in Method). Is there a way to make that Payment reflect “$0.00” immediately? My customers will be confused if they click on the View Payment and it still shows a balance owed, even though we charged their card already.  Method sales: Yes, we can customize this so it's $0 right away 
3.	Is it necessary to select  Payment Method  card type when the information is already saved on the next screen? Can we remove this step? What purpose does this required field serve, if it's already saved? Method sales: Yes, we can control this setting and remove this step.

- Donor snap charges based on Contacts and not based on users using their platform

- Right now they pay $40 to Method and moving forward they will pay $79 to DonorSnap for 5000 contacts limit (They have ~3000 now)

- They want to add 15 users to DonorSnap and they will still pay $79 in total per month
